carousels.hook_text: What if dinner was the one thing you didn't have to think about?

content_slides.headline: The mental load of dinner is real
content_slides.body_text: It's not just cooking. It's the deciding, the remembering, the managing everyone's preferences — every single day.

content_slides.headline: 5 decisions you make before 5pm
content_slides.body_text: Every weeknight, you're carrying all of this:
list_items: What does everyone actually like right now?
What's in the fridge vs. what needs to be bought?
Who has a practice / late meeting / meltdown tonight?
Is this fast enough for a Tuesday?

content_slides.headline: That's not indecision. That's overload.
content_slides.body_text: Decision fatigue from managing everything else makes dinner feel impossible — even when you're a great cook.

content_slides.headline: What it looks like when the system works
content_slides.body_text: The plan is already made. The groceries are already ordered. Your family already agreed. You just cook.

cta_slide.cta_secondary: Link in bio — get your first week planned free

carousels.caption:
The dinner question isn't actually about food. It's about everything else you're already carrying. 💙

You deserve a system, not more decisions.

carousels.hook_text: "What's for dinner?" is the most exhausting question in your house.

content_slides.headline: BEFORE: The nightly spiral
content_slides.body_text: 5:15pm. Fridge open. Nothing jumps out. You ask the family. They shrug. You suggest something. Someone complains. You make two things. Everyone's annoyed.

content_slides.headline: AFTER: The plan already exists
content_slides.body_text: 5:15pm. You know it's Tuesday. Tuesday is the lemon herb chicken. It's already in the fridge. Your family already voted yes on Sunday. You just cook.

content_slides.headline: The difference isn't willpower.
content_slides.body_text: It's not that some families are more organized. It's that some families have a system that removes the question entirely.

content_slides.headline: The question doesn't have to happen every night.
content_slides.body_text: Slated builds your weekly plan, sends it to your family to vote on, and pushes the grocery list to Instacart. The question gets answered once. On Sunday.

cta_slide.cta_secondary: Answer the question once. Link in bio.

carousels.caption:
Hot take: "What's for dinner?" shouldn't be a daily question. It should be answered once, on Sunday, by a system.

We built that system. 🍽️
carousels.sound_suggestion: Trending dramatic build — e.g. cinematic tension sound

carousels.hook_text: The 10-minute Sunday ritual that makes weeknight dinners disappear.

content_slides.headline: Step 1: Pick your 5 nights (2 min)
content_slides.body_text: Monday–Friday. Which nights are tight? Which nights do you have 30 minutes vs. 15? Map it before you pick meals.

content_slides.headline: Step 2: Match meals to nights, not vibes (3 min)
content_slides.body_text: Tuesday is soccer night → 15-minute meal. Thursday is WFH → can do 30 min. Stop picking meals in a vacuum.

content_slides.headline: Step 3: Send the plan to your family (1 min)
content_slides.body_text: Before you buy a single thing. Let them react. Adjust once. Now they have no grounds to complain mid-week.

content_slides.headline: Step 4: One grocery order, done (4 min)
content_slides.body_text: Combined list for all 5 nights. Order it or shop it. No mid-week "we're out of" emergencies.

content_slides.headline: That's the whole system.
content_slides.body_text: 10 minutes on Sunday. Zero dinner decisions Monday through Friday. Slated automates every step of this.

carousels.hook_text: Picky eaters aren't the problem. The planning process is.

content_slides.headline: The myth vs. the reality
content_slides.body_text: We've been solving the wrong problem for years.
content_slides.left_text: Your kid is just difficult and won't eat anything
content_slides.right_text: Your kid was never asked what they wanted before you cooked it

content_slides.headline: What actually causes dinner meltdowns
content_slides.body_text: The protest isn't about the food — it's about the surprise.
content_slides.left_text: Decide alone → cook → present → hear complaints
content_slides.right_text: Show options → let them pick → cook → no surprises

content_slides.headline: Buy-in before cooking changes everything
content_slides.body_text: When kids (and partners) vote on the plan before you shop, they've already said yes. Wednesday complaints evaporate.
content_slides.left_text: "I don't like this" at the table

content_slides.headline: Slated's Dinner Draft does this automatically
content_slides.body_text: It generates options based on your family's preferences, sends them a vote, and builds the week from the results. No negotiating at 6pm.
content_slides.left_text: You decide alone and hope for the best
content_slides.right_text: Family votes Sunday. Everyone's already agreed by Monday.

#PickyEaters #FamilyDinner #MentalLoad

carousels.hook_text: Nobody sees the 47 decisions you made before dinner hit the table.

content_slides.headline: It started at 2pm.
content_slides.body_text: You checked what was in the fridge. Mentally catalogued what needed to be used before it went bad. Cross-referenced everyone's schedule. Did math on cooking time.

content_slides.headline: Then came the research phase.
content_slides.body_text: Scrolled for a recipe that was fast, that your youngest would eat, that didn't require a grocery run, that wasn't the same thing you made last Tuesday.

content_slides.headline: Then the negotiation.
content_slides.body_text: "What do you want for dinner?" "I don't know." "How about X?" "Not that." "Y?" "Maybe." You made Y. Someone said they weren't hungry.

content_slides.headline: And nobody saw any of it.
content_slides.body_text: They saw a meal appear on the table. They didn't see the hour of invisible work that made it happen. Every. Single. Night.

content_slides.headline: This is the labor that deserves a system.
content_slides.body_text: Not more effort. Not more scrolling. A system that handles the deciding so you can just cook — or not cook — without the weight.

cta_slide.cta_secondary: The system exists. Link in bio.

carousels.caption:
The invisible labor of dinner isn't the cooking. It's everything that happens before the cooking. And it happens every single day. 💙

You're not alone in this.
carousels.sound_suggestion: Soft emotional piano or ambient — builds through slides

carousels.hook_text: 5 spring dinners that go from fridge to table in under 25 minutes.

content_slides.headline: Why spring is actually the easiest season to cook
content_slides.body_text: Lighter ingredients, shorter cook times, and produce that basically seasons itself. These five dinners are your new rotation.

content_slides.headline: 1. Lemon Herb Chicken Thighs
content_slides.body_text: One pan, 20 minutes. Bright, fresh, and the whole family eats it without negotiation. Serve over rice or with crusty bread.

content_slides.headline: 2. Shrimp & Snap Pea Stir Fry
content_slides.body_text: 15 minutes start to finish. High protein, kid-friendly sauce, and snap peas are peak right now. This is your Tuesday.

content_slides.headline: 3. Spring Pasta Primavera
content_slides.body_text: Asparagus, peas, lemon zest, parmesan. 20 minutes. Vegetarian-friendly and genuinely delicious — not just "healthy."

content_slides.headline: 4. Sheet Pan Salmon with Broccolini
content_slides.body_text: Into the oven, walk away for 18 minutes. One pan to wash. Omega-3s your family didn't know they were eating.

content_slides.headline: 5. Chicken Tacos with Mango Slaw
content_slides.body_text: Rotisserie chicken shortcut. The mango slaw takes 5 minutes and makes it feel like you tried. Everyone builds their own = zero complaints.

cta_slide.cta_secondary: Get all 5 in a ready-made plan — link in bio

carousels.caption:

carousels.hook_text: What if your family answered "What's for dinner?" before you asked?

content_slides.headline: The old way: you decide alone
content_slides.body_text: You pick the meals. You buy the groceries. You cook. Someone says they don't like it. You absorb the complaint. You do it again tomorrow.

content_slides.headline: The new way: the family decides together
content_slides.body_text: You send the plan options on Sunday. Everyone votes. The winning meals become the week. When Wednesday's dinner lands on the table, it was already agreed to.

content_slides.headline: What changes when they're involved
content_slides.body_text: Complaints drop. Eating improves. And the invisible labor of solo decision-making finally gets shared — even if you're still the one cooking.
list_items: No more "I didn't ask for this"
No more guessing what everyone wants
No more carrying the whole decision alone

content_slides.headline: This is what Slated's Dinner Draft does
